categories.name: Web Architecture
categories.description: Core strategies and patterns for building and delivering web applications.

topics.name: Rendering Fundamentals
topics.description: The basic process of converting code and data into viewable HTML.

topics.name: Static and Server Rendering
topics.description: Strategies involving pre-built files and server-side generation.

topics.name: Performance and Hydration
topics.description: Optimizing user experience through efficient JavaScript execution and loading.

questions.question_text: What is the technical definition of 'rendering' in the context of web development?
questions.explanation: According to the source, rendering is the specific process of turning data and code into HTML that the end user can see.
questions.hint: Think about the transformation from code to what the user sees.

choices.text: The process of uploading files to a storage bucket.
Turning data and code into HTML for the end user.
The execution of JavaScript within the browser console.
Pointing a domain name to a server IP address.

questions.question_text: What is the primary drawback of using a traditional static website rendering paradigm?
questions.explanation: Static sites are not suitable for websites where data changes often because they lack the ability to handle dynamic data without full rebuilds.
questions.hint: Consider sites with frequently updated information.

choices.text: They are expensive to host in storage buckets.
They cannot be programmatically built using frameworks.
They are not ideal for websites where data changes frequently.
They require a full server-side runtime like Django.

questions.question_text: Which two factors are identified as significant disadvantages of the Single Page Application (SPA) paradigm?
questions.explanation: SPAs require a large JavaScript bundle that slows initial loads and can cause SEO issues because search engines struggle with dynamic content routes.
questions.hint: Think about bundle size and search engine visibility.

choices.text: High server costs and lack of routing support.
Slow initial page loads and difficulty with SEO.
Requirement for full page reloads and clunky UI.
Inability to fetch data via HTTP requests.

questions.question_text: How does Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR) handle dynamic data differently than a standard static site?
questions.explanation: ISR allows developers to deploy a static site but rebuild individual pages on the fly on the server when a cache is invalidated based on specific rules (like time).
questions.hint: Focus on the 'incremental' and 'on the fly' aspects.
questions.answer_text: ISR allows for rebuilding individual static pages on the fly when the cache is invalidated, enabling dynamic data updates without a full site redeploy.

questions.question_text: What is the key advantage of the Islands Architecture, as used by frameworks like Astro?
questions.explanation: Islands architecture starts with static HTML and only hydrates specific interactive components, which means it may ship zero JavaScript to the client for non-interactive pages.
questions.hint: Consider what happens if a page has no interactive parts.

choices.text: It requires the entire page to be hydrated at once.
It can ship zero JavaScript to the client for purely static pages.
It eliminates the need for any HTML on the server.
It forces all components to be interactive by default.

learning_cards.title: Jamstack & SSG
learning_cards.front: What defines a 'Jamstack' site in the context of Static Site Generation (SSG)?
learning_cards.back: HTML is rendered in advance and uploaded to a static host, but it hydrates to JavaScript after the initial page load to provide an app-like experience.

learning_cards.title: Resumability (Quick)
learning_cards.front: How does the Resumability paradigm (Quick framework) differ from traditional hydration?
learning_cards.back: It serializes the website, data, and event listeners into HTML. JavaScript is broken into tiny chunks and lazy-loaded, avoiding the standard hydration process entirely.
